breaking_news.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Attack Compromises LiteLLM and Trivy
breaking_news.summary:
Threat actor TeamPCP executed a sophisticated multi-ecosystem supply chain attack compromising the Trivy security scanner and LiteLLM Python package. Malicious versions of LiteLLM were published on PyPI, embedding credential harvesters and Kubernetes lateral movement tools that targeted developer environments and CI/CD pipelines.

Over 1,000 SaaS environments have been infected, with the campaign now involving extortion group Lapsus$ to amplify the impact. This incident highlights severe risks in the AI software supply chain, exposing cloud credentials and API keys across thousands of organizations globally.

breaking_news.title: Coruna iOS Exploit Kit Linked to Operation Triangulation
breaking_news.summary:
Researchers discovered the Coruna iOS exploit kit, which reuses updated kernel exploit code from the 2023 Operation Triangulation campaign. The framework targets Apple iPhones running iOS versions 13.0 through 17.2.1 with five full exploit chains and 23 total exploits.

This development suggests a resurgence of state-sponsored surveillance capabilities previously linked to Operation Triangulation, posing a significant threat to high-value targets. While ineffective against the latest iOS release, the campaign demonstrates the persistence of advanced persistent threat actors in mobile espionage.

breaking_news.title: CISA Adds Langflow Vulnerability to Known Exploited Catalog
breaking_news.summary:
CISA added a critical vulnerability in the Langflow AI workflow tool to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog due to active exploitation. The flaw, CVE-2026-33017, allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary Python code via the public build endpoint without sandboxing.

Organizations using Langflow for agentic AI workflows face immediate risk of full system compromise and data theft. Security teams are urged to patch to version 1.9.0 or later to prevent attackers from hijacking AI agents and accessing sensitive infrastructure.

breaking_news.title: China-Linked Red Menshen Spies via Telecom Backbone
breaking_news.summary:
A China-linked threat cluster known as Red Menshen has embedded stealthy BPFDoor implants within telecom backbone infrastructure to conduct long-term espionage. The group maintains passive backdoors in critical environments, enabling high-level surveillance of government networks.

This campaign represents a significant escalation in state-sponsored cyber espionage targeting critical communications infrastructure. The strategic positioning allows the actors to monitor traffic and maintain persistent access without triggering traditional network defenses.

breaking_news.title: Critical Citrix NetScaler Flaw Similar to CitrixBleed
breaking_news.summary:
Citrix issued urgent patches for a critical NetScaler vulnerability, CVE-2026-3055, which shares similarities with the widely exploited CitrixBleed flaws. The out-of-bounds read vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote attackers to leak sensitive information from the appliance's memory.

With a CVSS score of 9.3, this flaw poses a severe risk to enterprises relying on NetScaler ADC and Gateway for identity and authentication. Immediate patching is required to prevent credential theft and potential lateral movement within corporate networks.

breaking_news.title: FCC Bans Foreign-Made Routers Over National Security Risks
breaking_news.summary:
The Federal Communications Commission officially banned the import of new consumer-grade routers manufactured outside the United States. This decision cites unacceptable risks to national security and communications networks, adding foreign-made routers to the Covered List.

The ban impacts the global supply chain for home networking equipment and forces manufacturers to seek exemptions from DHS or defense authorities. While aimed at mitigating supply chain risks, the move may leave consumers with older, less secure devices in the short term.

breaking_news.title: Lapsus$ Claims Breach of Pharma Giant AstraZeneca
breaking_news.summary:
The notorious cybercrime group Lapsus$ claimed responsibility for breaching pharmaceutical giant AstraZeneca, stealing approximately 3GB of sensitive data. The alleged leak includes internal code repositories, credentials, and employee information, though the company has not yet confirmed the breach.

If verified, this incident represents a major compromise in the healthcare sector, potentially aiding attackers in mapping systems and launching targeted phishing campaigns. The involvement of Lapsus$ suggests a coordinated extortion effort against critical infrastructure entities.
